title: Klue breach lead to Salesforce data theft, Huntress affected
blurb: A breach at market-intelligence platform Klue cascaded into Salesforce data theft at Huntress and other security vendors via stolen OAuth tokens.

title: FortiBleed: 86,000 Fortinet Device Credentials Compromised
blurb: CISA is urging organizations to harden internet-facing Fortinet devices after a credential-theft campaign dubbed FortiBleed compromised more than 86,000 firewalls and VPNs across 194 countries, roughly half of all internet-exposed Fortinet devices. A Russian-speaking threat actor intercepted SSL VPN authentication and cracked hashes on a 45-GPU cluster, fully compromising at least four organizations and pivoting into internal Active Directory environments.

title: CISA Warns Fortinet Customers as FortiBleed Hits 86,644 FortiGate Devices
blurb: CISA urged Fortinet customers to secure internet-facing FortiGate appliances against FortiBleed, a credential-spraying campaign attributed to Russian-speaking attackers that has compromised 86,644 devices as of June 19. Most breached logins were unrenamed default or built-in system accounts, and once inside, the attackers passively harvest fresh credentials to spread further, hitting telecom, government, and education hardest across India, the US, and Mexico.

title: Post-silicon era gets closer as industry giants crack the 2D transistor scaling bottleneck with breakthrough tech — imec, ASML, and TSMC fab complementary 2D-material transistors at 50nm pitch on a 300mm wafer
blurb: Imec, ASML, and TSMC fabricated complementary 2D-material transistors on a standard 300mm wafer at a 50nm contacted poly pitch, the tightest yet demonstrated and tighter than Intel's 10nm-class node. Using a single EUV exposure to print 28nm channels, the team reported 94% of devices switching correctly with an on/off ratio above 100,000, pairing MoS2 n-channels with WSe2 p-channels on production tooling.

title: Lutnick’s Anthropic Crackdown Claims New Power Over AI Models
blurb: The Commerce Department issued an order using export control laws to restrict access to Anthropic's AI models, an unprecedented application led by Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick. The move raises untested legal questions about whether the US government can dictate who is allowed to use domestic AI systems, setting up a likely court fight over the limits of export authority.

title: ASML denies US government report that its EUV chipmaking tool was shipped to China — says 'rumors' are 'inaccurate and damaging to our reputation'
blurb: ASML denied a Bloomberg report that US officials believe one of its EUV lithography systems reached China in breach of export controls, calling the claims unfounded and damaging to its reputation. The company says it knows the location of every EUV tool it has built and has never shipped an EUV machine, component, or specialized transport system to China, where such exports are barred under the Wassenaar Arrangement.

title: Two AIs just matched or beat doctors on diagnosis. The catch: none of the patients were real.
blurb: Two AI systems, Germany's Mira and Google's Gemini-based Amie, matched or beat doctors at diagnosis and treatment planning in Nature studies this week, with Mira hitting 87% diagnostic accuracy across 500+ emergency cases versus 78% for a panel of six physicians. Both were tested only on simulated, text-only patients with no exams or scans, and the models tested are roughly two years old, so the comparison flatters the AI more than real-world clinical conditions would.

title: Anthropic’s Fable and the State of AI
blurb: Schneier argues that AI capability gains are now driven as much by the open-source "harness" — the ordinary code that stitches models together and hands them tools — as by the models themselves, using a hypothetical export-control crackdown on a fictional Anthropic model to make the point. Because harness improvements need little data or compute, restricting any single model can't contain the broader rise in capabilities, which the author says demands collective action that isn't currently possible.

title: Waymo hits the brakes after robotaxis keep missing the signs for freeway construction zones
blurb: Waymo paused freeway robotaxi operations after its vehicles repeatedly failed to detect signage marking highway construction zones. The setback slows the company's push to expand beyond city streets onto faster roads, where missed lane closures and detours carry higher stakes than the surface-street driving Waymo has already scaled across several US markets.

title: UnitedHealth’s $3 Billion AI Push Has Bots Calling Doctors
blurb: UnitedHealth is spending $3B on AI that reads chart summaries to nurses, analyzes millions of customer calls, and in one trial deploys agents to call doctors' offices and schedule patient appointments. The company is betting automation can cut costs and ease the backlash over claim denials and service complaints that has dogged the insurer.

title: Forget traffic lights, Google’s reCAPTCHA may ask for hand gestures
blurb: Google added hand gesture verification to reCAPTCHA, analyzing video of 21 hand-knuckle coordinates to confirm a user is human.

title: Bcachefs exits experimental status in new 'performance release'
blurb: Bcachefs, the copy-on-write Linux filesystem, dropped its experimental label in a new release focused on performance improvements. The change signals that maintainer Kent Overstreet considers the on-disk format stable enough for wider use, though bcachefs remains outside the mainline kernel after its removal, so users still rely on the standalone DKMS module to run it.

title: So You Want to Define a Well-Known URI
blurb: Well-known URIs fit only when a client already knows the site and needs something site-wide; a registry slot confers no legitimacy or adoption.

title: New Texas power grid allocation framework could lift bitcoin miners turned data center operators
blurb: Texas regulators are weighing a new framework for allocating power grid capacity that could favor bitcoin miners that have pivoted to running AI data centers. ERCOT data shows data centers now make up nearly 90% of the state's 438 GW of large-load demand, giving operators with existing grid connections an edge as competition for power intensifies.

title: The US FERC approves new orders to fast-track data center power requests, aiming to handle them in 90 days, while bringing new requirements for AI hyperscalers (Bloomberg)
blurb: The US Federal Energy Regulatory Commission approved new orders to fast-track data center power requests, targeting 90-day turnaround on interconnection decisions. The rules also impose fresh requirements on AI hyperscalers, addressing grid-reliability concerns as their power demand strains utilities and crowds out other customers seeking capacity.

title: SK Telecom named as the Korean carrier at the center of Anthropic's Mythos export controls controversy — access was revoked days before White House took Mythos and Fable 5 offline for all foreign nationals
blurb: Wired identified SK Telecom, South Korea's largest carrier, as the company whose access to Anthropic's Claude Mythos model the White House ordered revoked over alleged China ties, days before the Trump administration's export controls pulled Mythos and Fable 5 offline for all foreign nationals. SK Telecom's own China footprint is minimal, but the concern attaches to parent SK Group, whose affiliates hold extensive Chinese semiconductor and energy interests.

title: Bernie Sanders’ AI bill would hand the public half of OpenAI, Anthropic and xAI
blurb: Bernie Sanders introduced a bill that would impose a one-time 50% tax, paid in stock, on AI firms with over $200M in annual AI sales, handing the public half of companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI. The shares would seed a roughly $7T sovereign wealth fund paying every American a $1,000 annual dividend, though a Republican-controlled Congress makes passage unlikely.

title: Waymo recalls self-driving software after cars enter closed freeway work zones
blurb: Waymo recalled its fifth-generation Automated Driving System after cars drove past ramp-closure signs and between cones into active freeway construction zones in Phoenix and the San Francisco Bay Area. The company has restricted freeway driving while it builds a software fix to detect and avoid closed work zones, adding to scrutiny from regulators weighing tighter rules for autonomous vehicles.

title: Telegram founder accuses Meta of sabotaging access in India with BGP hijacks
blurb: Telegram founder Pavel Durov accused Meta of using BGP hijacks to degrade Telegram access in India, alleging the rerouting of traffic amounts to deliberate sabotage of a competitor. Meta has not substantiated the claim, but the accusation escalates an ongoing rivalry and raises questions about internet routing integrity in one of the world's largest mobile markets.

title: What Amazon’s Astro Taught Me About Giving Robots a Soul
blurb: Amazon's lead UX sound designer for the Astro home robot explains how character must be designed before voice, motion, or behavior.

title: Converting Coal Plants to Natural Gas
blurb: Around 140 US coal plants have been converted to burn natural gas since 2008, while more than 200 others simply shut down as coal's share of US electricity fell from over 50% to roughly 16%. The piece examines what pushed operators to switch fuels rather than retire, even as the Trump administration forces plants to stay online and tries to fund new coal construction.

title: Ambani’s Jio Weighs India Satellite Network to Rival Starlink
blurb: Jio Platforms, controlled by Mukesh Ambani, is weighing plans to build its own satellite constellation to expand India's communications infrastructure. The move would position Jio against Elon Musk's Starlink, which has faced regulatory and licensing hurdles entering the Indian market.

title: Geopolitical jitters push Europe's internet registry away from cloud-first strategy
blurb: RIPE NCC, the regional internet registry for Europe and the Middle East, is reversing its cloud-first strategy and moving core systems back to self-hosted infrastructure over fears that geopolitical pressure could cut off access to US-based cloud providers. The shift reflects a broader European push for digital sovereignty, as organizations weigh the operational convenience of hyperscalers against the risk of losing control during a political dispute.

title: Trump kneecaps Anthropic, SpaceX bags Cursor and Databricks debuts AI agent coworker
blurb: The Trump administration banned Anthropic's latest models from foreign use, forcing the company to shut down access entirely and reversing its recent run of good fortune. In the same week, SpaceX used part of its IPO haul to acquire AI coding startup Cursor, Databricks launched Genie One agents it pitches as an "agentic coworker," and Salesforce bought customer-service startup Fin for $3.6B.

title: Bernie Sanders files bill proposing 50% public ownership of US AI firms and giving out $1,000 dividends — VP Vance says Trump supports giving the American people a stake in AI companies, prefers ‘pre-distribution’ over giving away cash
blurb: Senator Bernie Sanders filed the American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act, which would put 50% of US AI companies' voting shares under a bipartisan federal commission and pay every American a roughly $1,000 annual dividend from a fund his office values at $7 trillion. VP JD Vance said Trump backs giving the public a stake in AI firms but favors "pre-distribution" over cash payouts, a rare point of alignment between the two.

title: Memory crisis hits such extremes that 'even Apple can't be safe'
blurb: Apple plans "unavoidable" price increases on its products, with CEO Tim Cook citing an AI-driven memory shortage he called "unsustainable." Analysts expect roughly $100 hikes on the iPhone Pro and Pro Max plus most Mac and iPad models, as Nvidia's AI chips absorb the limited supply of memory and storage that a handful of vendors can produce.

title: 16-year-old SATA II SSD survives 1 petabyte of writes — 25x more than the drive's endurance rating
blurb: A 2010 SanDisk P4 SSD survived 1 petabyte of writes, 25x its rated TBW endurance, in a WolfyTech endurance test.

title: The Secret Revolution in Battery Technology: 3-D Printing
blurb: Startups are using 3-D printing to manufacture batteries in custom shapes, letting energy storage conform to the contours of a device rather than forcing products to be built around standard cells. The approach could embed power directly into wearables, medical implants, and other form factors where conventional rectangular and cylindrical cells waste space or do not fit.

title: Fable Converted Pylint to Rust
blurb: prylint is a Rust reimplementation of pylint that produces byte-for-byte identical output — same messages, lines, columns, exit codes, and score footer — verified against real pylint across 52 codebases and roughly 65,000 Python files. It runs a median of 85x faster, reproducing pylint's bugs and crash messages rather than fixing them, and installs with pip for drop-in use.

title: Chipmakers Renew Nerdy Performance Tussle That Nvidia’s Dominance Had Quashed
blurb: Chipmakers are reviving the benchmark wars over CPU performance, a marketing fight that faded as Nvidia's GPUs became the center of the AI buildout. With CPUs drawing renewed attention for AI workloads, rivals are again trading competing performance claims, signaling that the processor race extends beyond the GPU dominance that had defined the past several years.

title: Turkey approves Uber's $335M deal to buy Getir's delivery business, tied to a $500M investment pledge in Turkey; Uber is also paying $100M for a 15% Getir stake (Ana-Maria Stanciuc/The Next Web)
blurb: Turkey's competition authority approved Uber's $335M acquisition of Getir's delivery business, conditioned on Uber pledging $500M of investment in Turkey. Uber is separately paying $100M for a 15% stake in Getir, deepening its push into the country's grocery and food-delivery market.

title: Chinese university-led mission to study asteroid Apophis during close encounter with Earth
blurb: A spacecraft built by more than 20 Tsinghua University undergraduates, the $2.8M START smallsat, will study asteroid Apophis when it passes within 32,000 kilometers of Earth in April 2029. Launching on a Landspace Zhuque-3 rideshare in early 2028, the 200kg craft will close to within 7 kilometers of the 340-meter asteroid, joining flagship missions from Japan, ESA, and NASA at the same encounter.

title: Apple just said the thing about Siri that we’ve long wanted to hear
blurb: Apple's Siri AI overhaul, now in the iOS 27 beta, delivers a single consistent assistant across iPhone, Apple Watch, Mac, HomePod, and Apple TV. Apple's watchOS engineering director said the unified experience was an intentional goal, fixing the fractured capabilities that long made old Siri behave differently on each device.

title: Joanna Stern spent one week with new Siri AI, and it’s very good
blurb: Joanna Stern published a hands-on review of iOS 27's revamped Siri after a week of testing, concluding the AI assistant is now genuinely good. Her standout examples lean on Siri's new Personal Context features, which pull from Messages, Calendar, and voicemail to deliver tailored answers, while the video also flags persistent mistakes and guardrails around medical and romantic prompts.

title: US Presses ASML Over an EUV Machine It Says May Have Reached China
blurb: US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick told ASML that one of its EUV lithography machines, the only tools capable of printing the most advanced chips, may have reached China in violation of export curbs imposed since the first Trump administration. ASML denies any EUV system is in China, citing a document that counts 314 machines worldwide with none in the country and notes the 180-ton systems cannot be relocated without its involvement.

title: Buildings May Soon Have ‘Immune Systems’ That Fight Airborne Disease
blurb: The federal government is spending $150M to develop technology that keeps indoor air clean, giving buildings a kind of "immune system" against airborne pathogens. Scientists are pursuing approaches including germicidal UV light, improved filtration, and real-time pathogen sensors, aiming to cut transmission of viruses and bacteria in offices, schools, and hospitals.

title: Intel hires former SK hynix chief Seok-Hee Lee to lead Intel Foundry advanced packaging — company establishing section as 'focused business with dedicated leadership'
blurb: Intel hired former SK hynix CEO Seok-Hee Lee as executive vice president of Intel Foundry, splitting advanced packaging into a standalone business under his control while Naga Chandrasekaran narrows to front-end work on the 18A and 14A nodes. The move puts a former memory chief over the EMIB-T and HBI technologies Intel is ramping against TSMC's oversubscribed CoWoS, a unit whose foundry arm lost $10.3B on $17.8B of revenue in 2025.

title: Ukraine launches TrophyLab, turning captured Russian weapons into a battlefield R&D platform
blurb: Ukraine launched TrophyLab, a platform giving defense partners access to captured Russian weapons for study and physical testing to speed up countermeasures.

title: AI’s memory crunch just killed a phone. Even Apple flinched.
blurb: Nothing scrapped its planned CMF Phone 3 Pro, saying it could not build a budget phone that felt like a genuine upgrade with memory prices this high, the first device outright killed by AI's memory crunch rather than just made pricier. Apple is feeling it too, with Tim Cook calling the situation unsustainable as DRAM prices jumped roughly 90% in a quarter and CCS Insight projects global smartphone shipments to fall 15% in 2026.

title: Rocket Report: Rebuild begins at Blue Origin launch pad; Relativity targets Mars
blurb: Blue Origin has begun rebuilding the launch pad damaged in a recent mishap, while Relativity Space laid out plans aimed at eventually reaching Mars, in the latest edition of Ars Technica's Rocket Report. The roundup also notes Isar Aerospace scrubbed another Spectrum test flight over off-nominal fluid-system behavior, and SpaceX's next Starship flight could come as soon as next month with another suborbital path and Indian Ocean splashdown.

title: Epic Games unveils Launcher V2 in re-attempt to topple Steam, says redesigned storefront is up to 6.5x faster — promises player profiles, user reviews, universal controller support, and much more
blurb: Epic Games announced Launcher V2, a rebuilt storefront promising player profiles, user reviews, controller support, and faster load times within 12 months.

title: Lutnick’s Anthropic Crackdown Claims New Power Over AI Models
blurb: The Commerce Department issued an order targeting Anthropic that invokes export control laws to assert authority over who can access the company's AI models. The move marks an unprecedented application of those laws and raises legal questions about whether the federal government can lawfully dictate access to commercial AI systems.

title: Temporary Cloudflare Accounts for AI agents
blurb: Cloudflare introduced Temporary Cloudflare Accounts for Agents, letting an AI agent run `wrangler deploy --temporary` to ship a Worker, API, or site without first signing up, authenticating, or copying an API token. The deployment stays live for 60 minutes, during which a developer can claim it to make the account permanent, otherwise it expires automatically.

title: Anthropic’s Fable and the State of AI
blurb: Schneier argues that the US government's decision to classify Anthropic's Fable model as a munition and block foreign access won't contain AI risk, because the danger lies in the general climb of capabilities rather than any single model. He notes that open-source "harnesses," ordinary code that stitches models to tools, let cheaper models match Fable's vulnerability-finding performance, making export controls on one model largely ineffective.

title: Two AIs just matched or beat doctors on diagnosis. The catch: none of the patients were real.
blurb: Two new studies in Nature show specialist medical AI matching or beating doctors on diagnosis, though both were tested only on simulated patients. Germany's Mira hit roughly 87% diagnostic accuracy across 500+ emergency cases versus 78% for a six-doctor panel, while Google's Gemini-based Amie matched 21 UK GPs on reasoning and adhered more closely to treatment guidelines.

title: UnitedHealth’s $3 Billion AI Push Has Bots Calling Doctors
blurb: UnitedHealth is spending $3B on AI to cut costs and reduce customer backlash, deploying systems that read chart summaries aloud to nurses driving to home visits and analyze millions of customer calls to trace the roots of complaints. One trial has AI agents calling doctors' offices to schedule patient appointments directly.

title: ASML denies US government report that its EUV chipmaking tool was shipped to China — says 'rumors' are 'inaccurate and damaging to our reputation'
blurb: ASML denied a Bloomberg report that US officials believe one of its EUV lithography systems reached China in breach of export controls, calling the claims unfounded and damaging to its reputation. The company says it has never shipped an EUV machine or specially designed component to China and knows the location of every EUV tool it has built, while US officials cited undisclosed evidence of related equipment exports.

title: Waymo hits the brakes after robotaxis keep missing the signs for freeway construction zones
blurb: Waymo paused its freeway operations in parts of its service area after robotaxis repeatedly failed to detect signage marking active construction zones. The company said the vehicles struggled to interpret temporary lane shifts and worker-zone markers at highway speeds, underscoring how edge cases in road environments continue to limit fully driverless deployment.

title: The US FERC approves new orders to fast-track data center power requests, aiming to handle them in 90 days, while bringing new requirements for AI hyperscalers (Bloomberg)
blurb: The US Federal Energy Regulatory Commission approved new orders to fast-track data center grid connections, targeting a 90-day turnaround for interconnection requests. The orders impose fresh requirements on AI hyperscalers, including cost-sharing and reliability commitments meant to keep surging data center demand from straining the grid.

title: SK Telecom named as the Korean carrier at the center of Anthropic's Mythos export controls controversy — access was revoked days before White House took Mythos and Fable 5 offline for all foreign nationals
blurb: Wired identified SK Telecom, South Korea's largest wireless carrier, as the company whose access to Anthropic's Claude Mythos model the White House ordered revoked over alleged ties to China, days before the Trump administration imposed export controls that pulled the lab's most advanced models offline for foreign nationals. SK Telecom was one of roughly 150 organizations added to Anthropic's Project Glasswing in early June, the program that distributes Mythos for vulnerability detection.

title: Vercel debuts eve open source agent framework, tries to fix shadow AI with Passport
blurb: Vercel released eve, an open source framework for building AI agents, alongside Passport, a tool aimed at curbing shadow AI by governing how employees access models inside an organization. The company's CTO claims the cost premium of running on AWS indirectly through Vercel is offset by more efficient use of compute resources.

title: Bernie Sanders’ AI bill would hand the public half of OpenAI, Anthropic and xAI
blurb: Bernie Sanders introduced legislation that would impose a one-time 50% tax on the stock of large AI companies, paid in shares, handing the public a half-stake in firms like OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI and seeding a roughly $7tn sovereign wealth fund. The tax would apply to any AI firm with over $200mn in annual AI sales, and a seven-person commission would manage the fund and use its voting shares to block decisions it judges harmful.

title: Rights groups brand Home Office's AI age guesser for asylum-seekers as biased and inaccurate
blurb: Rights groups criticized the UK Home Office's plan to use facial age estimation on asylum-seekers, arguing the technology is biased and cannot reliably distinguish children from adults. Campaigners say the system is least accurate precisely at the child-adult boundary where the Home Office intends to deploy it, raising the risk that minors are wrongly classified as adults.

title: AI operating system startup Conduct closes £45m round
blurb: Conduct raised $60M (£44.72M) in a Series A co-led by Index Ventures and ICONIQ, with strategic investment from SAP, Creandum, Lucid Capital, and Booom. The startup's "AI operating system" maps how custom code, configuration dependencies, and integrations connect to business logic, aiming to make decades-old enterprise systems legible enough for AI agents to act on.

title: Bernie Sanders files bill proposing 50% public ownership of US AI firms and giving out $1,000 dividends — VP Vance says Trump supports giving the American people a stake in AI companies, prefers ‘pre-distribution’ over giving away cash
blurb: Bernie Sanders introduced the American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act, which would establish a fund holding 50% of voting shares in US AI companies and pay a roughly $1,000 annual dividend to every American. Sanders' office estimates the fund at $7 trillion, and VP JD Vance said Trump backs giving citizens a stake, though he prefers "pre-distribution" over cash payouts.

title: Trump kneecaps Anthropic, SpaceX bags Cursor and Databricks debuts AI agent coworker
blurb: Databricks introduced Genie One, a set of agents it calls an "agentic coworker," at its annual Data + AI conference, arguing that AI agents can become the enterprise system of record currently held by SaaS applications. CEO Ali Ghodsi positioned the company's data and AI platform as the foundation that gives those agents useful context, sharpening its rivalry with Snowflake.

title: Apple just said the thing about Siri that we’ve long wanted to hear
blurb: Apple confirmed in a TechRadar interview that Siri AI, the assistant's overhaul shipping in the iOS 27 beta, was deliberately built to behave identically across iPhone, Apple Watch, HomePod, Mac, and Apple TV. The change targets old Siri's long-standing weakness, where the assistant's capabilities were fractured by device, giving users one personalized Siri with consistent access to their data everywhere.

title: US Presses ASML Over an EUV Machine It Says May Have Reached China
blurb: US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick told ASML's leadership that one of its EUV lithography machines may have reached China in violation of export curbs, a claim ASML flatly denies. The company's Washington document counts 314 EUV systems worldwide with none in China, noting the 180-ton machines require constant ASML upkeep and cannot be relocated without its involvement. Shares fell 2.7% Friday.

title: A $5M tech-worker PAC is taking on Big Tech’s $100M war chest over AI rules
blurb: The Guardrails Alliance, a super PAC backing AI regulation, launched Thursday with roughly $5M from tech workers, labor unions, and parents, and aims to raise $15M this cycle. It faces Leading the Future, a $100M+ pro-AI PAC funded by Greg Brockman, Andreessen Horowitz, and Joe Lonsdale, with its first fight backing New York RAISE Act author Alex Bores in a congressional primary.

title: Lutnick’s Anthropic Crackdown Claims New Power Over AI Models
blurb: The Commerce Department's order against Anthropic leans on export control laws in a way they've never been used, claiming federal power to decide who can access frontier AI models. The legal question underneath is whether Washington can regulate AI the way it regulates chips and weapons, treating a software system as a controlled export. If the order holds, every US lab's release decisions become subject to government sign-off, and the precedent reaches any founder whose product depends on frontier model access.

title: Bernie Sanders files bill proposing 50% public ownership of US AI firms and giving out $1,000 dividends — VP Vance says Trump supports giving the American people a stake in AI companies, prefers ‘pre-distribution’ over giving away cash
blurb: Bernie Sanders filed the American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act, which would put 50% of the voting shares of every US AI company under a bipartisan federal commission and pay every American a roughly $1,000 annual dividend from a fund his office values at $7 trillion. The surprise is the alignment, with VP JD Vance saying Trump backs giving the public a stake, though he prefers pre-distribution over cash handouts. The bet underneath both positions is that AI concentrates wealth the way the industrial revolution did, and that the fight ahead is over who owns the upside, not whether the jobs survive.

title: The US FERC approves new orders to fast-track data center power requests, aiming to handle them in 90 days, while bringing new requirements for AI hyperscalers (Bloomberg)
blurb: The US FERC approved new orders to fast-track data center grid connections, targeting a 90-day turnaround on power requests while imposing fresh requirements on AI hyperscalers. The bottleneck for AI buildout has shifted from chips to electricity, and interconnection queues stretching years have become the real constraint on where compute gets built. For founders, the faster timeline favors whoever can site capacity near the grid now, while the new rules signal that hyperscalers will increasingly carry the cost and accountability for the load they add.

title: AI operating system startup Conduct closes £45m round
blurb: Conduct raised $60M in a Series A co-led by Index Ventures and ICONIQ, with strategic backing from SAP, to build an "AI operating system" for the bloated, heavily customized software that runs large enterprises. Decades of changes have left those systems opaque even to the people operating them, and that same opacity stops AI agents cold, because an agent can only act on a system it can understand. Conduct maps how every piece of custom code, config, and integration connects to the business logic it serves, betting that the unglamorous work of making legacy IT legible is where enterprise AI actually pays off.

title: Monument scores £18m seed for cloud banking platform
blurb: Monument Technology raised £18m in seed funding for its cloud Banking-Platform-as-a-Service, beating its initial £10m target. Founded in 2023, the UK firm stitches core banking, payments, and customer channels into one managed solution by integrating its own technology with existing components like Mambu, Salesforce, and Nice Actimize. The company has already opened a Series A at a higher valuation, a sign that demand for outsourced banking infrastructure is pulling capital forward even as financial institutions stay cautious about building these stacks in-house.

title: ‘Take Risk’: Canada Regulator Cuts Bank Capital Level to Boost Lending
blurb: Canada's banking regulator cut capital requirements for the country's largest banks for the first time in three years, freeing up balance sheet to fund a domestic push into defense, critical infrastructure, and AI. The explicit instruction to "take risk" marks a shift from the post-2008 reflex to keep raising buffers, betting that looser banks will channel credit toward strategic build-out. The move signals that AI and infrastructure capex is now a national-policy priority weighty enough for regulators to trade some financial-system caution to finance it.

title: Why Marketing Should Almost Never Report to Sales
blurb: Marketing rarely works reporting to sales, because sales burns budget for leads now and starves brand and longer-term plays.

title: Bernie Sanders’ AI bill would hand the public half of OpenAI, Anthropic and xAI
blurb: Bernie Sanders introduced a bill that would hit any AI firm with over $200M in annual AI sales with a one-time 50% tax paid in stock, handing the public a half-stake in OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI and seeding a roughly $7tn sovereign wealth fund. The fund would pay a 5% annual dividend, about $1,000 per American, while a Senate-confirmed commission used its voting shares to block decisions it judged harmful. It probably won't pass a Republican Congress, but it sets the most aggressive marker yet in a debate the labs themselves opened by floating voluntary public stakes.

title: Turkey approves Uber's $335M deal to buy Getir's delivery business, tied to a $500M investment pledge in Turkey; Uber is also paying $100M for a 15% Getir stake (Ana-Maria Stanciuc/The Next Web)
blurb: Uber got Turkish approval to buy Getir's delivery business for $335M, and the green light came tied to a $500M investment pledge in the country. Uber is also paying $100M for a 15% stake in Getir, deepening its bet on one of the original instant-grocery pioneers. The structure shows where cross-border M&A is heading: regulators in fast-growing markets increasingly price approval in local investment commitments, so the headline deal value is only part of what an acquirer actually signs up to spend.

title: AI health tech from ex-Palantir lead raises £12m
blurb: Frontier Health raised $16M (£12.09M) in seed funding led by Atomico to sell AI administrative tools into the NHS, founded by Palantir's former healthcare lead. Its product, JUNO, is an AI "teammate" that adapts to each Trust's existing processes and has already rolled out across several NHS Trusts, including East Sussex. The bet is that the biggest near-term wins in healthcare AI come from the unglamorous back office, cutting the manual admin that drives up waiting times, rather than from clinical diagnosis where the regulatory and liability bar is far higher.

title: Chinese backers move to buy Manus back from Meta at the original $2B price as revenue quadruples
blurb: Manus' early Chinese backers are reportedly moving to buy it back from Meta at the same $2B price, as revenue quadrupled to $400M-$500M.

title: Temporary Cloudflare Accounts for AI agents
blurb: Cloudflare launched Temporary Cloudflare Accounts for Agents, letting any AI agent run `wrangler deploy --temporary` to ship a Worker, API, or site with no signup, OAuth, or token copy-paste. The deployment stays live for 60 minutes, during which a human can claim it permanently or let it expire, giving background agents the throwaway write-deploy-verify target they need without a browser auth step.

title: Vercel debuts eve open source agent framework, tries to fix shadow AI with Passport
blurb: Vercel released eve, an open-source framework for building AI agents, alongside Passport, a tool meant to curb shadow AI by tracking and governing which models and agents employees run. The CTO concedes that going through Vercel to reach AWS carries a cost premium, but argues more efficient compute utilization offsets it.

title: A First Look at Scroll-Triggered Animations
blurb: Chrome 146 has shipped scroll-triggered animations, the first browser to do so, letting CSS play an animation for a fixed duration once an element crosses a scroll threshold rather than tying progress to scroll position. The new `timeline-trigger: view()` property handles the firing condition over a timeline range like `entry 100% exit 0%`, acting as a CSS-native equivalent to the JavaScript Intersection Observer API.

title: Bcachefs exits experimental status in new 'performance release'
blurb: Bcachefs, the copy-on-write Linux filesystem, has shed its experimental label in a new release that its maintainer is billing as a performance update. The milestone signals the filesystem is now considered stable enough for wider use, though it lands amid ongoing friction between bcachefs and the kernel maintainers over how changes flow into mainline Linux.

title: So You Want to Define a Well-Known URI
blurb: Mnot, co-author of the Well-Known URI spec and the registry's Designated Expert, lays out when a `/.well-known/` location is the right tool: cases where the client already knows the site and needs to discover something site-wide, like robots.txt or change-password. He warns against registrations sought for legitimacy or used as URL shorteners, since the latter locks you into a 1:1 service-to-site relationship.

title: Fable Converted Pylint to Rust
blurb: prylint is a Rust reimplementation of pylint that produces byte-for-byte identical output — same messages, lines, columns, exit codes, and score footer — verified against real pylint across 52 production codebases including django, numpy, and pandas. It runs 15–2300x faster, with a median speedup around 85x, and is a deliberate bug-for-bug port that reproduces pylint's crashes and quirks rather than fixing them.

title: Trump kneecaps Anthropic, SpaceX bags Cursor and Databricks debuts AI agent coworker
blurb: Databricks used its Data + AI conference to debut Genie One, a set of agents it pitches as an "agentic coworker," arguing that AI agents can become the enterprise system of record SaaS apps fill today, but only on top of a unified data platform. The framing sharpens its fight with Snowflake over which vendor owns enterprise context, positioning the lakehouse itself as the substrate agents query rather than another app in the stack.

title: A First Look at Scroll-Triggered Animations
blurb: Chrome 146 shipped scroll-triggered animations, the first browser to do so, using a new `timeline-trigger: view()` property that fires a fixed-duration CSS animation once an element crosses a scroll threshold. The post distinguishes them from scroll-driven animations, which sync progress to scroll position with no duration, and walks through the keyframes and timeline-range syntax with a 300ms background-fade demo.

title: What Amazon’s Astro Taught Me About Giving Robots a Soul
blurb: Amazon's lead UX sound designer for Astro argues that any device which moves through a home and turns toward you with intent will be read as a character, so the only choice is whether you shape that character or let it form by accident. He treats character as a design system, with sound, motion, and facial expression authored together as one emotional arc rather than bolted on after the hardware ships.

title: Unreal Engine’s AI push sparks backlash as developers fear the end of Blueprints
blurb: Epic Games confirmed that Blueprints, Unreal Engine's node-based visual scripting system, will be supported through UE6 Early Access but deprecated in later releases as the company pushes toward the Verse language and an expanding suite of AI-assisted production tools. The roadmap has unsettled indie and solo developers who rely on Blueprints for rapid prototyping, though Epic has set no removal timeline and frames its AI tooling as productivity aids rather than replacements.

title: BIG designs minimalist "home climate system" for Everyday
blurb: Everyday, a Copenhagen cleantech startup, partnered with BIG to design Air, a wall-mounted home climate system that combines heating, cooling, and air purification behind clean architectural lines and snap-on magnetic oakwood finishes. The pitch is that residential HVAC has been engineered and hidden but never designed, and a circular control panel running Everyday's EverydayOS learns household routines to manage temperature.

title: CISA: Splunk Enterprise flaw actively exploited, patch by Sunday
blurb: CISA ordered FCEB agencies to patch CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated flaw in Splunk Enterprise's PostgreSQL sidecar endpoint, by Sunday under BOD 26-04 after confirming active exploitation. The bug lets remote attackers create or truncate arbitrary files and has a public WatchTowr PoC chainable to RCE, affecting versions 10.0.0–10.0.6 and 10.2.0–10.2.3.

title: FortiBleed: 86,000 Fortinet Device Credentials Compromised
blurb: FortiBleed, a credential-theft campaign attributed to a Russian-speaking threat actor, has produced a verified database of 86,644 working credentials across 194 countries, roughly half of all internet-facing Fortinet firewalls per Shodan, with at least four organizations fully compromised via intercepted SSL VPN auth and Active Directory pivoting. CISA issued a Thursday alert urging customers to terminate active sessions, reset credentials, enforce phishing-resistant MFA, and lock down management access.

title: Unauthenticated RCE in Splunk Enterprise under active attack (CVE-2026-20253)
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated RCE in Splunk Enterprise, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, ordering federal civilian agencies to mitigate by June 21 after Splunk and Resecurity confirmed in-the-wild exploitation. The flaw lets attackers invoke file operations through an unauthenticated PostgreSQL sidecar endpoint to achieve full platform compromise, so patch to 10.2.4 or 10.0.7 and hunt for path-traversal requests and unexpected pg_dump activity.

title: CISA Warns Fortinet Customers as FortiBleed Hits 86,644 FortiGate Devices
blurb: CISA, NCSC, and SOCRadar are tracking FortiBleed, a credential-based campaign by Russian-speaking actors that has compromised 86,644 internet-facing FortiGate appliances by spraying leaked Fortinet logins, then passively sniffing traffic to harvest more valid credentials and pivot to additional devices. Default and built-in system accounts make up 63% of compromised credentials, so operators should rotate factory credentials, rename default admin accounts, and pull edge management interfaces off the public internet.

title: Klue breach lead to Salesforce data theft, Huntress affected
blurb: A supply-chain breach at market-intelligence platform Klue cascaded into Salesforce data theft at multiple security vendors, including Huntress, Recorded Future, Tanium, and Jamf. Attackers used a dormant API credential to access Klue's backend on June 11, pushed token-harvesting code to steal customer OAuth tokens for Salesforce, HubSpot, Slack, and other services, then queried connected CRMs directly; the extortion group "Icarus" is demanding payment, and Klue customers should revoke active integration sessions and review access logs.

title: Cybercriminals abused GitHub, YouTube and VirusTotal to push crypto-stealing malware
blurb: Check Point researchers uncovered a cryptocurrency-stealing campaign that disguised malware as crypto sniper bots and gambling "predictor" tools, using fake GitHub stars, forks, AI-narrated YouTube tutorials and favorable VirusTotal comments from coordinated "Ghost Network" accounts to fabricate legitimacy. The Rust-based clipboard hijackers target Windows and macOS, swapping copied wallet addresses for attacker-controlled ones drawn from a list of over 15,500 addresses across Bitcoin, Ethereum, Monero and other chains.

title: Forget traffic lights, Google’s reCAPTCHA may ask for hand gestures
blurb: Google added hand gesture verification to reCAPTCHA, prompting users to perform gestures on camera so the system can extract 21 hand-knuckle landmark coordinates to confirm they are human. Google says the videos are not tied to user identity, exclude audio, and are deleted after verification, while visual and audio challenges remain available for accessibility needs.

title: Anthropic’s Fable and the State of AI
blurb: Schneier argues that the US government's move to classify Anthropic's Fable cyber-offensive model as a munition and block foreign access misses the point, since export controls on a single model can't stop a general rise in AI capabilities. His sharper claim is that the "harness," ordinary code that chains models and tools, now drives much of that capability, and cheaper open-source harnesses have already matched Fable using smaller models.

title: Telegram founder accuses Meta of sabotaging access in India with BGP hijacks
blurb: Telegram founder Pavel Durov accused Meta of using BGP hijacks to disrupt Telegram traffic in India, alleging Meta-controlled networks announced routes for Telegram IP prefixes to blackhole or degrade access. The claim is unverified and Meta has not responded, but BGP route hijacking remains a viable censorship and disruption vector against any service lacking RPKI origin validation and route monitoring.

title: CryptoBandits Malware Doubles as a Backdoor, Abuses Tor
blurb: Microsoft warns of CryptoBandits, a Windows cryptocurrency clipper active since February 2026 that doubles as a script-based backdoor, combining clipboard theft, wallet seed-phrase extraction, screenshot exfiltration, and RCE while routing C&C traffic through a bundled Tor client over a local SOCKS5 proxy. Spread via malicious .lnk shortcuts and USB worming, it evades detection through runtime decryption, so defenders should harden script execution paths and monitor local SOCKS proxy abuse.

title: Everything's bigger and better in Texas – even data breaches
blurb: A breach at a third-party vendor serving Texas state government exposed personal data on roughly 3M hunting and fishing license holders. Organizations relying on the same vendor should confirm the scope of exposed fields, watch for license-holder data surfacing in credential and identity-fraud markets, and treat the incident as a reminder to audit downstream processors handling bulk citizen records.

title: This Week in Security: Arch AUR, Steam Marketplace, WordPress All Face Issues, Taco-Themed Coding, and Mythos Makes National News
blurb: Starting June 11, attackers hijacked over 1,500 abandoned Arch User Repository (AUR) packages by claiming maintainership and modifying build scripts to pull malicious NPM dependencies (atomic-lockfile, js-digest) that deploy infostealers harvesting browser credentials, SSH keys, repo tokens, and crypto wallets. The malware hides via process renaming, systemd persistence, and eBPF socket cloaking, and a second wave with heavier obfuscation hit NeoVim plugins and browsers on June 14.

title: Britain's privacy watchdog quits after 'poor judgment' admission
blurb: Britain's Information Commissioner resigned after admitting to a lapse in "poor judgment," vacating the office that enforces UK data protection and GDPR-equivalent rules. The departure leaves the regulator without a permanent head as it handles active casework on AI, age-assurance systems, and cross-border data transfers, raising questions about continuity in enforcement priorities until a successor is named.

title: CISA: Splunk Enterprise flaw actively exploited, patch by Sunday
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated Splunk Enterprise flaw, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities list and ordered federal agencies to patch by Sunday under BOD 26-04. The bug lets remote attackers create or truncate arbitrary files via an unauthenticated PostgreSQL sidecar endpoint, with WatchTowr proof-of-concept code already public and Shadowserver tracking over 1,400 internet-exposed Splunk instances.

title: Unauthenticated RCE in Splunk Enterprise under active attack (CVE-2026-20253)
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated RCE in Splunk Enterprise, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and ordered federal civilian agencies to apply mitigations by June 21, after Splunk and Resecurity confirmed in-the-wild exploitation. Because Splunk sits at the center of enterprise SIEM and monitoring, a full compromise can erase visibility, expose stored credentials, and let attackers pivot internally, making the June 10 patches to 10.2.4 or 10.0.7 urgent.

title: FortiBleed: 86,000 Fortinet Device Credentials Compromised
blurb: CISA urged organizations to harden internet-facing Fortinet devices after FortiBleed, a credential-theft campaign that compiled over 86,000 verified working logins from FortiGate firewalls and VPNs across 194 countries, roughly half the internet-exposed Fortinet fleet. A Russian-speaking actor intercepted SSL VPN authentication and pivoted into Active Directory, prompting CISA to recommend session termination, credential resets, PBKDF2 admin hashing, and phishing-resistant MFA.

title: CISA Warns Fortinet Customers as FortiBleed Hits 86,644 FortiGate Devices
blurb: CISA urged Fortinet customers to secure internet-facing FortiGate appliances against FortiBleed, an ongoing campaign by Russian-speaking actors that has compromised 86,644 devices as of June 19, spreading through credential spraying and passive traffic monitoring. With generic admin and default Fortinet accounts making up 63% of breached credentials, the campaign exposes how many enterprises never rotated factory credentials, hitting telecom, government, and education hardest.

title: Klue breach lead to Salesforce data theft, Huntress affected
blurb: A breach at market-intelligence platform Klue cascaded into Salesforce CRM data theft at multiple customers, including security vendors Huntress, Recorded Future, Tanium, and Jamf, after attackers used a dormant API credential to plant token-harvesting code that scraped OAuth tokens for Salesforce, HubSpot, Slack, and other integrations. The incident is a reminder that a single third-party integration credential can expose CRM data across an entire connected SaaS stack, putting integration-token governance on the procurement checklist.

title: Forget traffic lights, Google’s reCAPTCHA may ask for hand gestures
blurb: Google added hand gesture verification to reCAPTCHA, part of its Google Cloud Fraud Defense platform, prompting users to perform gestures on camera while the system extracts 21 hand-knuckle landmark coordinates to confirm they are human. Google says the videos are not tied to user identity and are deleted after verification, and it offers existing visual and audio challenges as a fallback for accessibility needs.

title: UnitedHealth’s $3 Billion AI Push Has Bots Calling Doctors
blurb: UnitedHealth committed $3B to deploy AI across its operations, using the technology to read chart summaries to home-visit nurses, analyze millions of customer calls, and pilot agents that call doctors' offices to book patient appointments. The spend signals how large payers are betting automation can cut administrative costs and blunt public backlash over claim denials, though regulatory scrutiny of AI in coverage decisions remains an open risk.

title: ASML denies US government report that its EUV chipmaking tool was shipped to China — says 'rumors' are 'inaccurate and damaging to our reputation'
blurb: ASML denied a Bloomberg report that U.S. officials, including Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, suspect one of its EUV lithography systems reached China in breach of export controls, calling the claims unfounded and damaging to its reputation. The company says it knows the location of every EUV tool it has ever built and has never shipped a machine or EUV-specific component to China, where SMIC's sole order still sits in the Netherlands.

title: Vercel debuts eve open source agent framework, tries to fix shadow AI with Passport
blurb: Vercel released eve, an open-source agent framework, alongside Passport, a governance layer meant to curb shadow AI by routing employee agent usage through sanctioned, auditable channels. For IT leaders, Passport is the more relevant piece, offering visibility and policy controls over unsanctioned AI tools, though it ties governance to Vercel's platform and its AWS-backed compute economics.

title: Everything's bigger and better in Texas – even data breaches
blurb: A third-party vendor handling Texas state outdoor-licensing data exposed the personal records of roughly 3M hunters and anglers, according to The Register. The incident underscores the supply-chain exposure governments inherit when they outsource citizen data to external processors, putting renewed scrutiny on vendor security requirements and breach-notification clauses in public-sector procurement contracts.

title: Rights groups brand Home Office's AI age guesser for asylum-seekers as biased and inaccurate
blurb: UK rights groups challenged the Home Office's AI age-assessment tool, arguing it cannot reliably distinguish minors from adults precisely at the age boundary where officials plan to deploy it for asylum-seekers. The dispute highlights the accuracy and bias liabilities of deploying facial-analysis AI in high-stakes public-sector decisions, where misclassification carries legal exposure and procurement scrutiny that private-sector buyers rarely face.

title: Google Workspace Weekly Recap - June 19, 2026
blurb: Google's Workspace recap details several admin-relevant additions, including discoverable spaces that sit between private and open Chat spaces, Carrier Link for adding certified local-carrier phone numbers via a pre-configured SIP Link, and Google Voice call recording with AI transcription and action items. Admins also gain new Gemini app controls over temporary chats and history deletion, plus Calendar API transfer and filter endpoints for managing secondary calendars.

title: Geopolitical jitters push Europe's internet registry away from cloud-first strategy
blurb: RIPE NCC, the regional internet registry for Europe and the Middle East, is reversing its cloud-first strategy and repatriating core systems to self-hosted infrastructure over concerns about data sovereignty and US-controlled hyperscalers. The shift signals that European institutions managing critical internet resources increasingly treat dependence on AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud as a geopolitical liability rather than a default, raising the cost of operating sovereign infrastructure.

title: Microsoft: June 2026 Windows updates break Recycle Bin prompts
blurb: Microsoft confirmed that its June 2026 security updates introduce a bug across all supported Windows client and server releases, causing the Recycle Bin's permanent-delete dialog to show internal filenames like $Rxxxxx.ext instead of the original names. A fix is pending in a future update, but for now affected organizations must contact Microsoft's Business Support team to obtain a workaround.

title: Memory crisis hits such extremes that 'even Apple can't be safe'
blurb: Apple CEO Tim Cook told the Wall Street Journal that AI-driven memory shortages will force "unavoidable" price increases across its products, with analysts at IDC and BofA expecting roughly $100 hikes on iPhone Pro models and most Mac and iPad lines. Nvidia's AI chips are absorbing nearly all available memory and storage output, leaving device makers to pay up or wait, and signaling sustained component-cost pressure across enterprise hardware refreshes.

title: OpenAI adds spend controls and usage analytics to ChatGPT Enterprise
blurb: OpenAI added spend controls and usage analytics to ChatGPT Enterprise, giving admins a Global Admin Console that breaks down ChatGPT and Codex credit consumption across users, products, and models and lets them set budgets per team. The tooling addresses cost governance as AI spend fragments across business units, though analysts note it tracks consumption without yet tying those costs to measurable business outcomes.

title: Intel hires former SK hynix chief Seok-Hee Lee to lead Intel Foundry advanced packaging — company establishing section as 'focused business with dedicated leadership'
blurb: Intel hired former SK hynix CEO Seok-Hee Lee as executive vice president of Intel Foundry and spun advanced packaging into a standalone business reporting to Lee, who now owns EMIB-T and HBI integration as Naga Chandrasekaran narrows to the 18A and 14A front-end nodes. The move targets the back-end work behind AI accelerators, where Intel is pitching its EMIB family against TSMC's oversubscribed CoWoS and reportedly courting Google and Amazon for custom-chip packaging.

title: Google, Microsoft offer specs to help you prove your AI is behaving nicely
blurb: Google, Microsoft, OpenAI, Arm, Mastercard, and Siemens launched the Appia Foundation, a Linux Foundation-hosted body that builds specifications to help enterprises assess whether their AI systems meet applicable standards and regulations. Appia explicitly won't set standards like ISO/IEC but offers a Requirements layer and an Assessment Enablement layer to bridge global rules with practical conformity checks across regions like the tightly regulated EU.

title: Microsoft's latest Windows bug belongs in the Recycle Bin
blurb: A new Windows bug tied to the Recycle Bin is the latest defect IT admins must track in Microsoft's patch backlog.

title: Siemens expects Xcelerator revenue to more than double in 2026, aiming to make the platform an industrial app store integrating software and hardware offerings (Marilen Martin/Bloomberg)
blurb: Siemens expects revenue from its Xcelerator platform to more than double in 2026, part of a push to turn the software portfolio into an industrial app store that integrates its software and hardware offerings. The strategy positions Siemens to bundle design, simulation, and operations tooling under one procurement, raising switching costs for industrial buyers already standardized on its PLM and automation stack.

title: Why the FDA’s new real-world evidence guidance ends the era of structured-data-only submissions
blurb: FDA's new real-world evidence guidance for medical devices requires clinical-fact-level data traceability, exposing how structured-only EHR pipelines miss critical signal.

title: Temporary Cloudflare Accounts for AI agents
blurb: Cloudflare launched Temporary Cloudflare Accounts for Agents, letting any AI agent run `wrangler deploy --temporary` to ship a Worker, API, or site without first clearing an OAuth flow, dashboard, or MFA prompt. The deployment stays live for 60 minutes and can be claimed into a permanent account or left to expire, giving background agents a throwaway target for their write-deploy-verify loop.

title: Unauthenticated RCE in Splunk Enterprise under active attack (CVE-2026-20253)
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated RCE in Splunk Enterprise, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after Splunk and Resecurity confirmed in-the-wild exploitation through an unauthenticated PostgreSQL sidecar endpoint. Federal civilian agencies must patch by June 21, and because Splunk anchors most SIEM and monitoring stacks, a compromise blinds detection and exposes stored credentials, so prioritize upgrading to 10.2.4 or 10.0.7 and hunt for path-traversal and pg_dump indicators.

title: Bcachefs exits experimental status in new 'performance release'
blurb: Bcachefs dropped its experimental label in a release its developers are calling a "performance release," signaling the copy-on-write filesystem is now stable enough for production workloads after years of in-kernel maturation. The status change matters for platform teams weighing it against established options like ZFS and Btrfs, since experimental flags typically gate adoption in environments where data integrity and predictable I/O latency are non-negotiable.

title: New CMA action to secure fairer Google searches for businesses
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority imposed two conduct requirements on Google search, ordering it to rank organic results, including AI Overviews, by objective, non-discriminatory criteria with advance notice of major changes, and to let users port their search data to third parties. Google has six months to comply on fair ranking and three on data portability, putting UK businesses on par with EU protections under the Digital Markets Act.

title: Franklin Templeton proposes new ETFs that turn corporate dividends into bitcoin
blurb: Franklin Templeton filed with the SEC for two ETFs, the US Equity Bitcoin DRIP Index ETF and US Innovation Bitcoin DRIP Index ETF, that hold 95% large-cap US equities and reinvest collected dividends into bitcoin to maintain a 5% allocation. If approved, the funds could begin trading as early as September, creating an automatic, dividend-funded source of steady bitcoin demand.

title: Ethereum could face core development ‘funding crisis’ within nine months, says former EF contributor
blurb: A former Ethereum Foundation contributor, VanEpps, warned that Ethereum core development could hit a funding crisis within three to nine months after the expiration of its Core Investment Program. The shortfall would hit client teams and protocol researchers who depend on EF grants, raising questions about how Ethereum sustains independent core development as treasury support narrows.

title: Franklin Templeton files for ETFs that reinvest stock dividends into bitcoin
blurb: Franklin Templeton filed for two ETFs that would reinvest equity dividends into bitcoin, with an effective date as early as Sept. 1, 2026. The structure routes recurring dividend income into BTC accumulation, extending the firm's crypto product lineup beyond its existing spot bitcoin and ether funds.

title: Base targets June 25 mainnet launch for Beryl upgrade and new B20 token standard
blurb: Base scheduled its Beryl upgrade to activate on mainnet June 25, introducing the native B20 token standard and cutting withdrawal delays from the standard seven days to five. The shorter exit window reduces capital lockup for users bridging assets back to Ethereum L1, while B20 gives builders a chain-native token primitive on the Optimism-based rollup.

title: Fidelity launches GENIUS-aligned money market fund for stablecoin issuers
blurb: Fidelity launched the Fidelity Reserves Digital Fund (FYMXX), a government money market fund that invests exclusively in reserve assets permitted for stablecoin issuers under the GENIUS Act, including Treasury bills, cash, and overnight repos. The fund joins recent GENIUS-aligned launches from State Street, BlackRock, BNY Mellon, and Goldman Sachs, all competing to custody reserves backing a stablecoin market that now sits near $315B.

title: Cybercriminals abused GitHub, YouTube and VirusTotal to push crypto-stealing malware
blurb: Check Point researchers uncovered a crypto-stealing malware campaign that abused GitHub, YouTube, SourceForge, and VirusTotal to make malicious trading bots and gambling "predictors" look trustworthy, using coordinated "Ghost Network" accounts to inflate stars, reviews, and favorable comments. The Rust-based clipboard hijackers swap copied wallet addresses for attacker-controlled ones drawn from a list of over 15,500 addresses across Bitcoin, Ethereum, Monero, and other chains.

title: Morgan Stanley files amendments for ETH and SOL ETFs, revealing lowest fees in market
blurb: Morgan Stanley filed amendments for its proposed spot ETH and SOL ETFs, disclosing fee structures that come in below any competing product currently on the market. The amendments signal active SEC dialogue and progress toward launch, positioning the bank to undercut incumbent issuers on cost as spot altcoin ETFs move closer to approval.

title: CFTC enters settlement with former Celsius CEO, imposes a permanent trading ban
blurb: The CFTC settled its enforcement case against former Celsius CEO Alexander Mashinsky, imposing a permanent ban on trading in CFTC-regulated markets. Mashinsky, already serving a 12-year prison sentence after pleading guilty to fraud, faces the trading ban as the regulator closes out the agency's action tied to Celsius's 2022 collapse.

title: CFTC, SEC request public comment to clarify definition of ‘swaps’ amid CME lawsuit
blurb: The CFTC and SEC jointly requested public comment on how to define "swaps," a move that could determine whether perpetual futures fall under either agency's jurisdiction. The request follows CME Group's Thursday lawsuit against the CFTC, which challenges the agency's decision to classify perpetual futures as futures contracts rather than swaps.

title: New Texas power grid allocation framework could lift bitcoin miners turned data center operators
blurb: Texas's grid operator ERCOT proposed a new large-load allocation framework that would govern how data centers, which make up nearly 90% of the state's 438 GW of large-load demand, connect to the grid. The rules could favor bitcoin miners that have pivoted to AI and data center operations, giving them clearer terms for securing power capacity in the country's largest crypto-mining market.

title: JPMorgan says bitcoin mining economics have ‘worsened’ as BTC trades below production cost
blurb: JPMorgan estimates bitcoin's production cost at roughly $78,000, well above the current spot price near $62,500, leaving miners underwater on freshly minted coins. The bank flagged worsening mining economics that pressure operators to sell reserves, curtail hashrate, or seek non-BTC revenue streams to cover energy and hardware costs.

title: ‘Most difficult day in the history of digital credit’: Strive CEO says leverage liquidation drove STRC and SATA selloff
blurb: Strive's STRC and SATA tokens, both designed to trade near a $100 par value, sold off sharply on Thursday before partially recovering. CEO Matt Cole attributed the drop to forced leverage liquidations rather than a fundamental break, calling it the most difficult day in the history of digital credit.

title: CryptoQuant says bitcoin microtransactions now make up 80% of daily transactions
blurb: Bitcoin transactions below 0.01 BTC now account for roughly 80% of daily transactions, up from about 44% in 2023, pushing network activity to its highest level since late 2024 even as prices stay weak, according to CryptoQuant. The firm attributes the surge to OP_RETURN data inscriptions from Runes, Ordinals, and BRC-20 protocols, which have driven the mempool to 128,000 unconfirmed transactions and could raise fees for time-sensitive transfers.

title: Rep. Steil introduces bill to block lawmakers from placing prediction markets bets on public policy issues
blurb: Rep. Bryan Steil introduced legislation barring members of Congress and their immediate families from placing prediction-market bets on policy outcomes they can influence. The bill targets platforms like Kalshi and Polymarket, which have expanded political-event contracts since regulators cleared US election markets, and would close a conflict-of-interest gap as lawmakers increasingly trade on the same legislation they vote on.

title: Kalshi holds early IPO talks with investment banks: report
blurb: Kalshi has held early, informal talks with investment banks about a potential IPO, The Information reported, as the prediction-markets platform's annualized revenue surpassed $2B, up from a $1B run rate in March. The talks follow a $1B Series F in May that valued Kalshi at $22B, even as the sector faces mounting state lawsuits and CFTC jurisdictional fights over sports and gambling-style contracts.

title: What Is the CLARITY Act? The Crypto Market Structure Bill Explained
blurb: The CLARITY Act, formally the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, is a proposed US bill that would set a federal framework for issuing, trading, and regulating digital assets, including which agency oversees which tokens. The legislation aims to resolve the SEC-CFTC jurisdictional split that has pushed many crypto firms to operate offshore or under enforcement uncertainty.

title: Sen. Gillibrand’s son is building a perps exchange that wants dual CFTC, SEC oversight: report
blurb: The American Perpetuals Exchange Corporation, founded by Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand's son, raised $30M at a $300M valuation and is seeking joint oversight from both the CFTC and SEC, according to a Fortune report. The dual-regulator structure would distinguish the venue from offshore perps platforms like Hyperliquid that operate outside US jurisdiction.

title: GoMining challenges Jack Dorsey's Square with payments system designed around bitcoin
blurb: GoMining released an SDK and APIs for GoBTC Pay, its bitcoin payment protocol that settles transactions directly on the Bitcoin network using the Stratum V2 mining protocol, with merchants paying 0.2% fees and receiving BTC rather than fiat by default. The design positions GoMining against Block's Square, which routes bitcoin payments over Lightning and converts to dollars unless the merchant opts to retain BTC.

title: World Cup Mints Big Prediction Winners and Million-Dollar Losers
blurb: World Cup betting on Polymarket and Kalshi has surged as the tournament turns prediction markets into a public ledger of seven-figure wins and losses. The high-profile activity is pulling fresh volume into the sector, accelerating an industry that has scaled rapidly since election-cycle trading proved its mainstream appeal.

title: Franklin Templeton proposes new ETFs that turn corporate dividends into bitcoin
blurb: Franklin Templeton filed with the SEC for two ETFs that hold 95% U.S. equities and 5% bitcoin, automatically reinvesting equity dividends into bitcoin exposure. The DRIP-structured funds create a steady, low-maintenance demand feed for bitcoin funded entirely by dividends, and could begin trading as early as September if approved.

title: CFTC, SEC request public comment to clarify definition of ‘swaps’ amid CME lawsuit
blurb: The CFTC and SEC jointly requested public comment on how to define "swaps," after CME Group sued the CFTC for classifying perpetual futures as futures contracts rather than swaps. The classification carries real stakes for venues like CME, since swaps fall under stricter clearing, margin, and reporting regimes that would reshape how perpetuals can be listed and traded in US markets.

title: Fidelity launches GENIUS-aligned money market fund for stablecoin issuers
blurb: Fidelity launched the Fidelity Reserves Digital Fund (FYMXX), a government money market fund whose shares are designed to back stablecoin reserves under the GENIUS Act, holding only eligible assets like T-bills, cash, and overnight repos. The launch follows State Street, BNY Mellon, Goldman Sachs, and BlackRock into a fast-growing competition to custody the $315B stablecoin market's reserves, with USDT alone holding 59% of supply.

title: Base targets June 25 mainnet launch for Beryl upgrade and new B20 token standard
blurb: Base set June 25 to activate Beryl on mainnet, an upgrade that introduces the B20 native token standard and cuts withdrawal delays from seven days to five. Faster exits narrow the capital-lockup gap that has kept some treasury and payments teams wary of L2 settlement, while B20 gives builders a Base-native primitive for issuing tokens directly on the chain.

title: Franklin Templeton files for ETFs that reinvest stock dividends into bitcoin
blurb: Franklin Templeton filed with the SEC for two ETFs that would reinvest the dividends paid by their stock holdings into bitcoin, with an effective date as early as Sept. 1, 2026. The structure pushes BTC exposure deeper into mainstream equity products, letting traditional dividend income accrue as crypto without investors directly buying or custodying the asset.

title: JPMorgan says bitcoin mining economics have ‘worsened’ as BTC trades below production cost
blurb: JPMorgan estimates bitcoin's production cost at roughly $78,000 against a spot price near $62,500, leaving miners underwater on every coin.

title: Morgan Stanley files amendments for ETH and SOL ETFs, revealing lowest fees in market
blurb: Morgan Stanley filed amendments for its proposed spot ETH and SOL ETFs, disclosing fee structures that come in below every competing product on the market. The additional filings signal ongoing dialogue with the SEC and forward movement toward launch, while the aggressive pricing sets up a fee war as issuers compete for flows in the still-young crypto ETF category.

title: ‘Take Risk’: Canada Regulator Cuts Bank Capital Level to Boost Lending
blurb: Canada's banking regulator, OSFI, lowered the Domestic Stability Buffer for the country's six largest banks for the first time in three years, freeing up capital to expand lending toward defense, infrastructure, and AI. The cut signals regulators prioritizing growth over caution, giving banks room to extend credit at a moment when domestic policy is pushing for heavier investment.

title: Kalshi holds early IPO talks with investment banks: report
blurb: Kalshi has held early, informal talks with investment banks about a potential IPO, The Information reported, with the prediction-markets platform now past $2B in annualized revenue, up from a $1B run rate in March. The IPO chatter lands amid intensifying scrutiny, as states like Kentucky sue Kalshi over alleged unlicensed sports betting while the CFTC asserts exclusive federal oversight.

title: ‘Most difficult day in the history of digital credit’: Strive CEO says leverage liquidation drove STRC and SATA selloff
blurb: Strive's STRC and SATA tokens, designed to trade near $100 par, sold off sharply Thursday before partially recovering, with the CEO blaming leverage liquidation.

title: What Is the CLARITY Act? The Crypto Market Structure Bill Explained
blurb: The CLARITY Act, formally the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, is a proposed U.S. bill that would set a federal market-structure framework for how digital assets are issued, traded, and regulated, including which agency oversees them. The bill aims to divide jurisdiction between the SEC and CFTC and clarify compliance rules for crypto firms, a long-standing gap that has pushed several to operate offshore.

title: Rep. Steil introduces bill to block lawmakers from placing prediction markets bets on public policy issues
blurb: Rep. Bryan Steil introduced a bill barring members of Congress and their families from placing bets on prediction markets tied to public policy outcomes. The legislation lands as platforms like Kalshi and Polymarket expand event-contract trading on political events, raising conflict-of-interest concerns and signaling tighter regulatory scrutiny of the fast-growing prediction-market sector.

title: Monument scores £18m seed for cloud banking platform
blurb: Monument Technology raised £18m in seed funding for its cloud Banking-Platform-as-a-Service, exceeding an initial £10m target and combining core banking, payments, and customer channels into a single managed solution that integrates Mambu, Salesforce, and Nice Actimize. The UK provider, founded in 2023, has already opened a Series A at a higher valuation, signaling demand from financial institutions for outsourced, end-to-end banking infrastructure.

title: Analysis: From Roboadvisors to AI Advisors at Coinbase and Mercury
blurb: Coinbase launched an SEC-registered AI investment advisor that can analyze portfolios and suggest trades, while Mercury shipped Command, an AI banking assistant across a platform serving 300,000+ businesses. The author argues AI is commoditizing financial analysis itself, leaving proprietary data, distribution, and product infrastructure as the durable competitive moats rather than the models.

title: GoMining challenges Jack Dorsey's Square with payments system designed around bitcoin
blurb: GoMining released an SDK and APIs for GoBTC Pay, its bitcoin payment protocol that settles transactions on-chain via the Stratum V2 mining protocol and pays merchants in BTC rather than fiat by default. The approach contrasts with Block's Square, which converts bitcoin to dollars at the point of sale, and charges 0.2% per transaction split between wallet providers and miners, with settlement averaging around 12 hours.

title: Ambani’s Jio Could Be India’s Biggest IPO
blurb: Jio Platforms is preparing what could be India's largest-ever IPO, drawing on a subscriber base of nearly 525 million and backers including Meta, Alphabet, and KKR. A listing would give global investors direct exposure to India's digital-payments and telecom buildout, where Jio's scale underpins UPI transaction volume and the country's mobile-financial-services growth.

title: World Cup Mints Big Prediction Winners and Million-Dollar Losers
blurb: World Cup betting has turned regulated prediction markets like Kalshi and Polymarket into a public ledger of outsized wins and million-dollar losses, drawing fresh volume into the fast-growing sector. The tournament is accelerating mainstream adoption of event contracts, intensifying scrutiny from the CFTC over whether sports-outcome trading falls under commodities regulation or state gambling law.

title: Warren raises €10M to fix Belgium’s broken workplace pensions
blurb: Warren raised €10M in a seed round led by Motive Ventures to build a flat-fee Belgian workplace pension fund, supervised by the FSMA, that charges employers a subscription and passes every cent of investment return to employees. The Ghent-based fintech is betting ahead of any mandatory-saving reform, having signed about 100 companies in a year against a market where median pension reserves for employees aged 56 to 65 sit below €10,000.
